summary: The PCPNDT Act of 1994 prohibits prenatal sex determination for the purpose of sex-selective abortion in India.
The PCPNDT Act permits sex determination if requested by the parents.
The PCPNDT Act was enacted in 2010.
NIPT detects cell-free fetal DNA in maternal blood with approximately 99% sensitivity for trisomy 21, but it is a screening test requiring confirmatory invasive testing.
NIPT is a definitive diagnostic test that does not require confirmation.
NIPT analyses fetal cells, not cell-free DNA.
Amniocentesis is performed at 15-18 weeks of gestation and involves sampling amniotic fluid containing fetal cells for karyotyping.
Amniocentesis is performed at 8-10 weeks of gestation.
Amniocentesis samples placental tissue, not amniotic fluid.
CVS (chorionic villus sampling) can be performed earlier than amniocentesis, at 10-12 weeks, by sampling trophoblastic tissue from the placenta.
CVS is performed after 20 weeks of gestation.
CVS has a lower miscarriage risk than amniocentesis.
In Down syndrome screening, serum AFP is decreased and hCG is increased; this pattern is a key component of the triple and quadruple tests.
In Down syndrome, both AFP and hCG are elevated.
AFP levels are not useful in Down syndrome screening.
Increased nuchal translucency on first-trimester ultrasound is an important screening marker for chromosomal abnormalities including trisomy 21, 18, and 13.
Nuchal translucency is measured in the third trimester.
Decreased nuchal translucency suggests chromosomal abnormality.
The triple test combines maternal serum AFP, hCG, and unconjugated estriol; in Down syndrome, AFP and estriol are low while hCG is high.
The triple test uses AFP, TSH, and albumin.
In the triple test for Down syndrome, all three markers are elevated.
